title: mfa — outbound webhooks
description: MFA DISPATCHES no outbound webhooks. Exposing MFA lifecycle events to tenant-configurable webhook endpoints would leak security-critical metadata to third parties — attestation formats, credential-model identifiers, sign_count values, and any other WebAuthn material carries a nonzero risk of assisting a targeted attack. Tenants that need MFA-lifecycle visibility can subscribe to the redacted `activity_log` stream via the observability module, which emits AGGREGATE counts + timestamps only.

inbound_webhook_kinds.name: fido_mds.metadata_updated
inbound_webhook_kinds.description: Optional FIDO Alliance Metadata Service integration — MDS publishes an update feed of authenticator model changes (new certifications, revocations, known-compromised keys). Received event triggers a re-scan of the WebauthnCredential table for AAGUIDs that just appeared on the revocation list. Not enabled by default in Wave 1a.
inbound_webhook_kinds.notes: When configured, a WebauthnCredential referencing an AAGUID that lands on the MDS revocation list is auto-disabled + WebauthnCredentialDisabled(reason=admin_force_disable) fires. Best-effort: MDS polling backs up the webhook.

rationale: The FIDO Alliance Metadata Service inbound webhook is defensive — cryptographic devices can have their certification revoked (bug in the secure element, private-key extraction, etc.). Auto-disable is the safe posture; the user is emailed via WebauthnCredentialDisabled → notification and asked to re-register with a different device.

For tenants that legitimately need cross-system MFA sync (e.g. an SSO federation announcing 'this user just registered a new passkey via SAML'), the correct integration path is via SCIM (deferred to Wave 1c per design.md §12). Webhooks would create a permanent leak of authenticator metadata to third parties who inevitably keep it forever.
